description: Seed physical-properties database for Integrity engine. Representative real-world values with cited sources. This flat file is the v0 seed; it will migrate to the Postgres source of truth (docs/05) and grow a material taxonomy + modules (docs/06, docs/07). See docs/04-materials-model.md for the schema.
provenance_note: Each material lists key source URLs. Best-constrained fields: density, moduli, Poisson, strengths, hardness. Weakest/estimated (labeled in notes): restitution, friction-vs-other-materials, ductility scalar, and optical fields except albedo. Optical values are tunable art parameters derived from cited reflectance/albedo. Full per-value citations will live in the Postgres DB.

optical.grain: isotropic to weakly anisotropic; coarse phaneritic 1-10mm; high-contrast quartz/feldspar/mica speckle

tillotson.source: Melosh 1989 App. II (via SWIFT/WoMa) & Benz et al. 1986 (via Meier/Reinhardt & Stadel 2021); three code reproductions agree. rho0=2680 is Melosh's (Benz uses 2700). Not read from the primary book.

materials.notes: Cohesion is intact rock; for fractured/granular state use cohesion~0 and friction_angle~40. Rejected bad MatWeb UCS (2200 MPa). Polished roughness ~0.05-0.15.

optical.grain: fine aphanitic, near-isotropic, sub-mm crystals, low-moderate contrast, optional vesicles

tillotson.source: Benz & Asphaug 1999, Icarus 142, Table 2 (CONFIRMED verbatim; params for lunar gabbroic anorthosite per O'Keefe & Ahrens 1982a, with basalt rho0 & bulk modulus). A=B=26.7 GPa, E0=487, E_iv=4.72, E_cv=18.2 MJ/kg, alpha=beta=5

materials.notes: Vesicular scoria much lighter (500-2200). Rock-mass strength far below intact. Polished slab roughness ~0.3.

optical.grain: coarse olivine/pyroxene, dark green-brown

tillotson.source: olivine/dunite Tillotson set, Marinova et al. 2011 (via the Stewart-group pyKO EOS table; single-source, not read from Marinova directly)
tillotson.notes: Peridotite modeled with the olivine (dunite) Tillotson set, the standard giant-impact mantle material. CORRECTED 2026-07-22 by sourcing: B was 4.9e11 (10x too stiff) and E0 was 5.5e7 (10x too low) -- transcription errors that made a differentiated body puff up. Marinova's values are B=49 GPa, E0=550 MJ/kg. Alternative: Chau et al. 2018 substitute rho0=3320 into basalt's moduli (a density hack, not an independent fit).

materials.notes: Earth's mantle rock (docs/25 layered planets). Surface-condition properties; melt_point is the peridotite SOLIDUS (~1120 C at 1 atm). Simon-Glatzel fit anchors the solidus at ~4150 K @ 135 GPa (CMB) per Fiquet et al. 2010. Deep-mantle density reaches ~5500 kg/m3 under compression - the layered-planet profile carries the compressed mean density separately (PREM).

optical.grain: anisotropic along bedding; 0.06-2mm sand grains; medium-high contrast; cross-bedding streaks, iron-oxide banding

materials.notes: Weakly cemented bulk hardness ~3-4 (grains Mohs 6-7). Disaggregated sand friction_angle ~30-35. Wet lowers strength and roughness (~0.4).

optical.grain: weak bedding anisotropy; fine-medium 0.1-2mm; low-moderate contrast; fossils/stylolites

materials.notes: Soft calcite (Mohs 3). Jointed cohesion 2-6 MPa. Rejected bad MatWeb UCS (700 MPa). Alabaster/onyx-marble translucency up to ~0.5.

optical.grain: isotropic two-scale: coarse aggregate 2-20mm over fine paste; medium-high contrast on fractured faces

materials.notes: Plain concrete near-brittle (failure strain ~0.0035); ductility comes from rebar, not concrete. Paste hardness ~2-3, aggregate 6-7 (composite). Rubble friction_angle 26-42.

materials.assumed_condition: low-carbon mild steel (AISI 1018 / ~ASTM A36); pure iron rarely used structurally

optical.grain: weak directional along rolling/machining axis; fine; low contrast

tillotson.source: compressed branch (rho0, a, b, A, B, E0): Wissing & Hobbs 2020, A&A 635 A21, Table 2 (refit to Brown et al. 2000, CONFIRMED verbatim); vapor branch (E_iv, E_cv, alpha, beta): Melosh 1989 iron (W&H did not refit these)
tillotson.notes: Planetary core iron. Compressed branch verified; vapor branch (E_iv/E_cv/alpha/beta) provisional and only bites at impact energies.

materials.notes: compressive_strength = compressive yield (ductile metals flow, not crush). friction_angle N/A (not granular). Brinell 126 HB, Vickers 131 HV. friction_coefficient is steel-on-steel dry. Optical albedo is measured metal F0; roughness/grain are art params. color_variance rises with rust.

materials.assumed_condition: annealed commercially-pure nickel (Ni 200); meteoritic iron is a Fe-Ni alloy, ~5-20% Ni

optical.grain: fine, low contrast; takes a bright polish

materials.notes: Commercially-pure Ni 200 (annealed). Iron meteorites are Fe-Ni (kamacite ~5-7% Ni, taenite up to ~50%). specific_heat/latent heats are elemental nickel.

optical.grain: weak directional along drawing/rolling axis; fine; low contrast when clean

materials.notes: Annealed (soft) temper; strength rises sharply when work-hardened. compressive_strength = yield. Brinell ~40 HB, Vickers ~50 HV. friction_coefficient is Cu-on-Cu (galls); Cu-on-steel ~0.53. color_variance rises with patina.

optical.grain: anisotropic along roll/extrusion axis; sub-mm to mm; low-moderate contrast (brushed higher)

materials.notes: 6061-T6 temper. Soft 1100-O far weaker (tensile ~8.8e7, Brinell 23). compressive_strength = yield. Brinell 93-95 HB. friction_coefficient Al-on-mild-steel static (kinetic ~0.47); Al-on-Al galls 1.05-1.4. Albedo ~92% neutral silver.

materials.assumed_condition: White Oak (Quercus alba) at 12% moisture content; strongly anisotropic

optical.grain: STRONG directional; coarse open-pore ring-porous; longitudinal streaks + prominent quartersawn ray fleck; high contrast

materials.notes: Anisotropic: along-grain (_perp suffix = across-grain) modulus ~14x cross; tensile ∥ ~15x ⊥ (dominant failure). Values at 12% MC; wet wood weaker. Hardness: Janka 6000 N. Finished-surface roughness ~0.35. Albedo linear (sRGB ~0.57,0.45,0.30).

materials.assumed_condition: Eastern White Pine (Pinus strobus) at 12% MC; anisotropic. Structural southern yellow pine is much stronger.

optical.grain: subtle straight grain (diffuse, low contrast) punctuated by dark high-contrast knots; fine even texture

materials.notes: Anisotropic (_perp = across grain). Soft but brittle in tension/across grain; low toughness. Southern yellow pine analog: density ~570, MOE ~12.3e9, MOR ~88e6, Janka ~3100 N. Albedo linear (sRGB ~0.85,0.73,0.50).

materials.assumed_condition: living ground cover over root-reinforced moist topsoil; modeled as vegetated soil, not a solid

thermal.estimated: organic + moisture; chars/vaporizes easily like water/biomass; not separately cited — impact vaporization, docs/24

optical.grain: directional blade-scale streaks (mm wide, cm long), anisotropic sheen when mown; ~isotropic at distance

materials.notes: Two stiffnesses coexist: soft soil mat (~MPa) vs stiff strong blades (blade tensile ~108-294 MPa, GPa modulus). Signature property is added ROOT cohesion ~1-20 kPa over few-kPa base soil. Restitution near zero. Fragile, shreds easily. Highly variable.

materials.assumed_condition: moist, loosely compacted loamy topsoil (silt/clay/sand + organics)

thermal.estimated: from silicate (loam) composition, ~parent rock; not separately cited — for impact vaporization, docs/24

optical.grain: fine-to-medium granular, clumpy; aggregates, roots, organic flecks, irregular mottling

materials.notes: Varies 2-3x+ with moisture/organics/compaction. Dry sandy dirt -> ~0 cohesion, brittle; compacted clayey -> stronger, plastic. Hardness N/A (loose aggregate). Wet friction ~0.40, wet albedo ~40-50% darker. Angle of repose ~35 moist.

materials.assumed_condition: moist, medium-stiff (firm) inorganic clay near plastic limit

thermal.estimated: from silicate (clay mineral) composition, ~parent rock; not separately cited — impact vaporization, docs/24

optical.grain: very fine, smooth/plastic and smeary when wet; polygonal shrinkage cracks when dry

materials.notes: Most moisture-sensitive common soil. Undrained cohesion c_u ~= UCS/2. Effective drained friction ~20 (undrained ~0). Desiccated clay becomes brittle (ductility ~0.2, cracks). Wet clay slippery (friction ~0.25). Dry hardness ~Mohs 1-2.

materials.assumed_condition: quartz sand, dry, loose-to-medium packed (0.1-1mm grains), cohesionless

thermal.estimated: quartz (SiO2); melt/boil of silica; not separately cited — impact vaporization, docs/24

optical.grain: fine matte granular with faint quartz-facet glints; uniform fine speckle

materials.notes: Cohesionless: no unconfined compressive strength; strength lives in friction_angle (loose 28-34, dense 40-46). Moist sand gains temporary apparent cohesion ~1-5 kPa (vanishes dry/saturated). Hardness 7 = quartz grains (bulk flows, N/A). Bulk restitution 0.2 (single grains 0.4-0.9). Wet albedo <0.20.

thermal.estimated: parent rock (granite/basalt); not separately cited — impact vaporization, docs/24

optical.grain: coarse chunky faceted pebbles; visible size/color variation; occasional mineral glint

materials.notes: Cohesionless; strength in friction_angle (angular interlock -> higher than sand). Hardness/color depend entirely on parent rock (limestone ~3, quartzite/granite 6-7); bulk N/A. Stiffer than sand.

optical.grain: none - smooth, uniform, specular

tillotson.source: SWIFT/WoMa 'Melosh 1989' planetary-SPH water set (cross-checked across code reproductions; a competing Woolfson 2007 set also exists). A=2.18 GPa matches water's real bulk modulus.

materials.notes: Liquid: bulk_modulus + dynamic_viscosity replace solid mechanics; strengths/friction/hardness/ductility N/A. Peak density 1000 at 4 C. Viscosity halves 0->40 C. Specular F0 ~0.02 (n~1.33). Restitution ~0 (splashes).

optical.grain: transparent medium; blue appearance is Rayleigh scattering (docs/26), not a surface albedo

materials.notes: Dry air at 15 C, 1 atm (docs/26 atmosphere-as-matter). specific_heat is c_p; specific gas constant R_s = R_u/molar_mass = 287 J/(kg K); gamma = 1.4 (diatomic) -> isentropic bulk modulus K = gamma*P. melt/boil are N2-dominated (63 K / 77 K at 1 atm) for cryogenic honesty. As a GAS its resistance to compression is the EOS (K = gamma*P), never an elastic modulus.

optical.grain: self-luminous plasma; appearance is EMISSION (blackbody ~5772 K photosphere), never reflectance

materials.notes: Solar-composition plasma (X~0.73 H, Y~0.25 He; mean molecular weight ~0.61 g/mol ionized). density is the SUN'S MEAN (M/V) - the layered profile (planet::sun) carries the real compression from ~150,000 kg/m3 core to ~2e-4 photosphere, exactly as PREM does for Earth. Estimates flagged.

materials.assumed_condition: generic light sweet crude, ~35 API (SG ~0.85); density at 15.6 C, viscosity/optics ~20 C

optical.grain: none - oily specular sheen; thin-film iridescence possible

materials.notes: Liquid. Game-relevant contrast with water: less dense (~850) yet ~6-10x more viscous, optically much darker/less translucent. Viscosity falls sharply as it warms. F0 ~0.02-0.025 (n~1.45-1.5).

materials.assumed_condition: carbon-black-filled SBR/NR tread compound at 20 C; vulcanised, ~60 Shore A

optical.grain: matte black, fine moulded texture; tread blocks are geometry, not grain

materials.notes: Nearly incompressible (poisson ~0.49): bulk modulus is ~300x Young's, so it changes SHAPE not volume — a tyre's contact patch spreads rather than compressing. friction_coefficient is the most condition-sensitive field here and is NOT a constant: dry road 0.7-1.0, wet 0.4-0.6, ice <0.15, and it FALLS with both temperature and slip speed (the reason a locked wheel stops gripping). Grip is hysteretic and load-dependent, not Amontons-Coulomb, so mu is a first-order stand-in — flagged. High ductility (elongation at break 400-600%) and restitution well below a bouncy ball because tread compounds are deliberately damped (loss tangent ~0.1-0.3), which is why tyres do not ring. THERMAL DELIBERATELY OMITTED: rubber does not melt, it PYROLYSES (~570-670 K) — an irreversible chemical change, so melt_point/latent_fusion have no honest value. The schema's optional `thermal` is how it says 'not characterised' (oak, concrete and ice do the same), and damage.rs then returns Fractured rather than ever claiming melt. Glass transition ~210-220 K: below it the compound goes glassy and grip collapses — real, and not yet representable without a temperature-dependent mu.

materials.assumed_condition: solid dense polycrystalline freshwater ice, 0 to -10 C

optical.grain: polycrystalline, coarse, glassy clear-to-bluish; columnar (lake) to granular (snow-ice)

materials.notes: Strengths vary ~4x with temperature and strain rate. Hardness rises sharply when colder (1.5 at 0C -> 6 at -78.5C). Brittle at high strain rate, ductile creep near melt (glacier flow). Friction is the most condition-sensitive field: kinetic min ~0.02-0.05 near -7C, rises near 0C and with speed; static ice-on-ice 0.1-0.5.

tillotson.source: Benz & Asphaug 1999, Icarus 142, Table 2 (CONFIRMED verbatim; ice params O'Keefe & Ahrens 1982b, rho0/bulk-modulus Lange & Ahrens 1983). alpha=10, beta=5 -- NOT the switched 5/10 some code copies carry.

materials.assumed_condition: settled/compacted natural snowpack ~300 kg/m^3, 0 to -10 C. MOST variable material here.

optical.grain: rounded well-bonded fine-medium grains (settled); powder = loose dendrites; firn = coarse rounded

materials.notes: Nearly every mechanical property scales with density and changes 2-4 orders of magnitude between fresh powder (~50, negligible strength) and firn (>550). Youngs modulus and compressive strength roughly follow (rho_snow/rho_ice)^2 - ALWAYS state assumed density. Ductile creep (slow) vs brittle slab fracture (fast). Albedo 0.8-0.9 fresh, 0.5-0.7 aged, spectrally flat.

optical.grain: transparent medium

materials.notes: CO2 at 0 C, 1 atm. THE atmosphere of Mars (~95% by volume, ~6 mbar surface) and the dominant gas left over a solidified magma ocean (~100 bar on the Hadean Earth, Zahnle et al.). At one atmosphere CO2 has NO liquid phase: it sublimes at 194.7 K, and the 216.6 K melt point applies only above the 5.18 bar triple point - which is why Mars grows CO2 FROST rather than rain. Refractive index (n-1) = 4.49e-4 at STP, ~1.5x air's, so a CO2 atmosphere scatters more per molecule than air does.
sources: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th ed.

optical.grain: transparent medium

materials.notes: SO2 at 0 C, 1 atm. The form volcanic sulfur takes at HIGH temperature (H2S dominates only in cool gases), so it is the sulfur species over a magma ocean - and it is a gas there, not the sulfuric acid of Venus's cloud deck, which needs cool altitudes to condense. Also present in Io's tenuous atmosphere and as surface frost.
sources: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th ed.

optical.grain: transparent medium

materials.notes: N2 at 0 C, 1 atm. 78% of modern air by volume and ~2.6% of Mars's; nearly inert, so it accumulates as more reactive species are lost. The `air` entry's melt/boil points are these, since air is N2-dominated.
sources: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th ed.

optical.grain: transparent medium

materials.notes: CH4 at 0 C, 1 atm. A reduced-atmosphere species: prominent in the early Hadean's hydride-rich air and on the giant planets and Titan. Zahnle et al. put CO/CH4 at 1:1 near 1000 K at one bar and 1200 K at 100 bar, so which of the two dominates is a temperature and pressure question.
sources: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th ed.

optical.grain: transparent medium

materials.notes: H2 at 0 C, 1 atm. The lightest gas, which is the point: at 2 g/mol its scale height is 14x air's, so it puffs a planet's atmosphere out and escapes readily - the reason Earth kept its nitrogen and lost the hydride-rich primary atmosphere it started with.
sources: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th ed.
